locked_facts: Net power radiated: P_net = eσA(T⁴ - T₀⁴)
When T > T₀: body loses heat by radiation (P_net > 0)
When T = T₀: thermal equilibrium — body radiates and absorbs equally (P_net = 0)
Greenhouse effect: Earth absorbs visible light from Sun, re-emits infrared, greenhouse gases trap IR
A body both emits and absorbs radiation simultaneously — net exchange determines heating/cooling
Prevost theory of heat exchange: all bodies at all temperatures radiate thermal energy
Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O) are transparent to visible but absorb infrared radiation
truth_statements: A body at any temperature above absolute zero continuously emits thermal radiation
The net rate of heat transfer by radiation depends on the difference between T⁴ of the body and T⁴ of the surroundings
At thermal equilibrium a body still radiates but absorbs exactly as much as it emits so the temperature stays constant
The greenhouse effect keeps Earth about 33°C warmer than it would be without an atmosphere
Enhanced greenhouse effect due to increased CO₂ and other gases is causing global warming

aha_moment.what_to_show: A hot body in cooler surroundings: it emits at rate eσAT⁴ AND absorbs at rate eσAT₀⁴ simultaneously. The NET exchange P_net = eσA(T⁴ - T₀⁴) determines cooling. At equilibrium (T = T₀): emission = absorption, net = 0. The body doesn't stop radiating — it just balances.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two radiation arrows (emitted and absorbed) with net arrow showing the difference — at equilibrium both are equal
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think cold bodies don't radiate and hot bodies only emit — seeing simultaneous emission and absorption with the net balance is the insight

simulation_states.label: Every body radiates
simulation_states.description: A body at temperature T radiates P = eσAT⁴. Even a cold body radiates — just less. A body at room temperature (300K) radiates ~460 W/m² (infrared).
simulation_states.emphasis: ALL bodies radiate at ALL temperatures — not just 'hot' objects

simulation_states.label: Net radiation exchange
simulation_states.description: Hot body (T) in cooler surroundings (T₀). Body emits eσAT⁴ and absorbs eσAT₀⁴. Net loss: P_net = eσA(T⁴-T₀⁴). If T < T₀: body gains heat.
simulation_states.emphasis: The NET exchange determines whether the body heats up or cools down

simulation_states.label: Greenhouse effect
simulation_states.description: Sun's visible light passes through atmosphere → absorbed by Earth → Earth re-emits infrared → greenhouse gases absorb IR → re-radiate back to Earth → warming. Without this: Earth would be ~-18°C.
simulation_states.emphasis: Atmosphere is transparent to visible but traps infrared — that's the greenhouse effect

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts body temperature and surroundings temperature. Net radiation power calculated. Cooling curve shown. Greenhouse gas concentration slider shows enhanced warming.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change temperatures — see net radiation reverse direction when body is cooler than surroundings

common_misconceptions.correction: Every object above 0 K radiates thermal energy continuously. An ice cube at 273 K radiates about 315 W/m². The radiation is in the far infrared and invisible, but it's there. At thermal equilibrium, it absorbs exactly as much as it emits.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show radiation arrows coming from a cold object — the rate is less but not zero

common_misconceptions.belief: The greenhouse effect is entirely bad/unnatural
common_misconceptions.correction: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — it keeps Earth at ~15°C instead of -18°C. The problem is the ENHANCED greenhouse effect from excess CO₂ emissions, which is causing additional warming beyond the natural balance.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show Earth temperature with no atmosphere (-18°C) vs natural greenhouse (+15°C) vs enhanced (rising)
